data.content: This is an MSTest project on .NET 8. I'll start `dotnet watch test` which will automatically re-run your tests whenever you save a file change.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

data.reasoningText:
The user has an MSTest project targeting .NET 8. They want to use hot reload (`dotnet watch test`) to iterate faster on failing tests. Let me set that up.

First, let me run `dotnet watch test` in async mode so they can see the output and iterate.

But before that, let me check - does `dotnet watch test` work with MSTest? Yes, `dotnet watch test` will re-run tests when files change.

Let me start it up.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-eddcdd89f2834f57a6c403ad661ed8a1 && dotnet watch test --no-restore 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for hot reload", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

data.content: The project has a build error — missing `using System;`. Let me fix that so `dotnet watch test` can run, then start hot reload.
data.reasoningText:
There's a build error - `ArgumentException` needs `using System;`. Let me also check - the test expects `CalculateShipping("express", 1.0)` to return `15.00m`, but the implementation returns `10.00m`. That's the failing test.

But the user asked me to set up hot reload, not fix the tests. Let me fix the build error first (missing `using System;`) so the project compiles, then set up `dotnet watch test`.

Wait, actually the user said "I have some failing tests and want to use hot reload to iterate faster." So they want me to set up the hot reload workflow. 

I'll resolve the compilation issue and get `dotnet watch test` running so they can iterate on the failing tests in real time.
